mnemonics.title: Caesar's CPK Formula
mnemonics.content: C = P + K (mod 26): Ciphertext = Plaintext + Key. For decryption: P = C - K. Think 'Caesar Plus Key' for encryption.

mnemonics.title: 25 KEYS for Caesar
mnemonics.content: Caesar has only 25 possible keys (1-25). Remember: TWO-FIVE makes it trivial to BRUTE-FORCE.

mnemonics.title: ETAOIN SHRDLU
mnemonics.content: The most common English letters in order: E, T, A, O, I, N, S, H, R, D, L, U. This sequence is the key to breaking monoalphabetic ciphers via frequency analysis.

mnemonics.title: MONO vs POLY
mnemonics.content: MONOalphabetic = ONE alphabet (Caesar, random substitution). POLYalphabetic = MANY alphabets (Vigenere). Mono preserves frequencies; Poly hides them.

mnemonics.title: 26! is Big but Broken
mnemonics.content: 26 factorial = huge keyspace but STILL breakable. Big key space does NOT mean security. Frequency analysis bypasses brute-force entirely.

keyPoints: Caesar cipher shifts each letter by a fixed key value K using C = (P + K) mod 26.
Only 25 possible keys make Caesar trivially breakable by brute-force.
Monoalphabetic cipher has 26! keys but is still broken by frequency analysis.
Both Caesar and monoalphabetic ciphers preserve plaintext letter frequencies.
These weaknesses motivated the development of polyalphabetic ciphers.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the mod 26 wrap-around when shifting past Z.
Assuming 26! keys makes monoalphabetic cipher secure (frequency analysis defeats it).
Confusing monoalphabetic (one fixed alphabet) with polyalphabetic (multiple alphabets).
